Steven Gundry:	You're probably wondering, what is leaky gut? How are all these different health issues related, and what do they have to do with my digestive tract? Get ready, because I'm about to explain everything. Leaky gut is a term used to describe a simple problem with a devastating result, that the lining of your digestive tract may be leaky, allowing dangerous toxins to sneak into your body, and wreck havoc on virtually every aspect of your health. Here's a simple way to understand leaky gut. When you eat some delicious food, it travels through your body along your digestive tract. Along the way, that food gets broken down in your stomach, and the good stuff gets absorbed into your system to provide you with nutrients and energy you need to survive, and the bad stuff, the junk like bacteria, toxins, and things that are dangerous to you, get processed, and well, you get rid of them when you go to the bathroom. It's our body's beautiful, natural system for making sure you get the most out of food, while easily and efficiently getting rid of the bad stuff.

Steven Gundry:	For the most part, your body is designed pretty well, but here's the problem. Your digestive tract is very long. Amazingly, if you could take it out and look at it, your GI tract would have the same square footage as an entire tennis court, and yet, despite being this large, it's very thin. In fact, the lining of your digestive tract is only one cell thick. It's the thinnest thing that you can imagine. Here's what's cool about your body. Even though the lining of your digestive tract is razor thin, these cells are really tough. You can think of them like a like of Secret Service agents, locking arms, making sure nobody bad can break through your gut wall, and for the most part, nobody does break through, but here's the bad part. Thanks to new research by myself and others, we now know there's one dangerous thing that not only can break through. It's actually really good at destroying your gut lining.

Steven Gundry:	They're called lectins. If you've seen my other videos, you may have heard me discuss lectins before, but I have new information on lectins that's sending shock waves through the medical community, which is why this is the most important video I've ever made. You see, lectins are tiny little proteins, found in certain kinds of foods, that are not supposed to be in your body. One famous type of lectin you may have heard of is gluten, but that's just one type. There's actually many found in various plants. Some common foods containing lectins are whole grain wheat, eggplants, and tomatoes. Many people have been told these are health foods, but in my opinion, they are anything but, because your ancestors didn't eat any of these so-called health foods. You see, tomatoes and eggplants weren't introduced to Western civilization until 1492, when Christopher Columbus discovered them in the New World and brought them back to Europe.

Steven Gundry:	Of course, he had no idea that they were packed with lectins, or the impact that they could have on human GI tracts, which had never been exposed to these foreign foods before. In the case of whole grain wheat, humans have been eating it for a while longer, since farming began about 10,000 years ago, but even then, most societies historically removed the dark wheat husk and instead ate white bread, which is not a coincidence, because it's in the dark husk that gut destroying lectins are contained. However, since about 1950, the use of whole wheat health foods have skyrocketed in our society, as well as other lectin-rich foods. Do you know what else has happened since 1950? They leave a hole in the protective gut lining, meaning other bad stuff can now get through, too. This, in short, is what we call leaky gut. Lectins making holes in your digestive lining.

Steven Gundry:	I wish I could say that that's where it ends, but actually, it's only the beginning of the problem. There are about five pounds of them in your gut, and the Secret Service agents in your cells are supposed to keep them out. Once you've got leaky gut, these endotoxins see the hole, and barge through, and when they do, watch out. These endotoxins storm across your entire body like an invading horde. If these endotoxins get into your joints, they can cause aches and pain. If they get into your skin, they can cause itchiness, redness, acne, and rashes. Steven Gundry:	Marshmallow root, which is not the puffy candy you eat around the campfire, but the actual root it's made from is well-known for its ability to coat and sooth the mucus membranes, including the holes in your gut.
